EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms impacted the Great Basin producing widespread light to moderate snowfall. Localized heavy snow occurred in Winnemucca and Golconda Summit, where 11-16 inches of snow was observed. Additionally, heavy snow fell across southern Lander and Eureka Counties. Snow amounts observed were 11-14 inches in Austin and Eureka. The heaviest snow fell from March 14th to 15th in central Nevada, and March 16th to 17th for northwestern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy snow developed across southern Lander and Eureka Counties during the morning and afternoon of March 14th. Snow continued in southern Lander county into the 15th. Austin received 6.5 inches, with a two day total of 10 inches. Eureka picked up 7.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Nevada. Some of the heavier snowfall amounts included 10 to 20 inches in Spring Creek and Lamoille, 14-17 inches in Eureka, and 7 inches in Ely. In addition, high winds gusting to 65 mph with the storm caused some property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported over much of the area. Spring Creek and Lamoille reported between 10 and 20 inches, and 6 to 8 inches were reported in Elko and Carlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Nevada. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were damaged in Ryndon, leading to power outages. Strong wind gusts were also reported in Spring Creek, with small tree branches snapped and shingles blown off houses. A large tree fell and destroyed a storage shed, from strong winds in Starr Valley. Estimated winds were between 50 MPH and 60 MPH for these events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Nevada. Some of the heavier snowfall amounts included 10 to 20 inches in Spring Creek and Lamoille, 14-17 inches in Eureka, and 7 inches in Ely. In addition, high winds gusting to 65 mph with the storm caused some property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snow fell across northern Elko County, with the heaviest snow observed in the mountains and along State Route 225 from Adobe Summit through Owyhee. Montello also received significant snowfall in the eastern portion of the zone. Widespread 7 to 12 inches of snow fell in the valley locations, with 10-15 inches in the mountains. The snow was particularly heavy in Owyhee, where a frontal band stalled creating snowfall rates in excess of 1-2 inches per/hr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms impacted the Great Basin producing widespread light to moderate snowfall. Localized heavy snow occurred in Winnemucca and Golconda Summit, where 11-16 inches of snow was observed. Additionally, heavy snow fell across southern Lander and Eureka Counties. Snow amounts observed were 11-14 inches in Austin and Eureka. The heaviest snow fell from March 14th to 15th in central Nevada, and March 16th to 17th for northwestern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very localized heavy snow band formed and remained stationary over extreme southeast Humboldt County, including Golconda Summit and the town of Winnemucca. Significant localized snow amounts ranged from 11 inches around Winnemucca to 16 inches near Golconda Summit. This was a historic snow event for Winnemucca, breaking snowfall records.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the northeast Pacific on the 18th moved southeast, reaching northern California on the 19th. This system brought high winds and snowfall to the Sierra Nevada and western Nevada. Lake-enhanced snow showers developed the evening of the 19th behind the departing system. This enhancement was mainly confined to areas immediately southeast of Lake Tahoe, but the snow showers extended well south into Mono County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 8 inches of snowfall at Dagget Pass (elevation 7334 feet) via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating and ample moisture along the periphery of a ridge caused scattered convection over western Nevada and the eastern Sierra July 19th through the 25th. Several storms produced heavy rainfall which led to flash flooding and debris flows. Hail was also reported with some storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nevada Department of Transportation reported that State Route 338 was closed in both directions from the California State Line to approximately Lyon County milepost 20 due to multiple debris flows. State Route 338 was closed from approximately 20 July 1313PST to 1948PST. No damage/cleanup estimates were available.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet atop Slide Mountain (elevation 9650 feet) measured a 107 mph wind gust with 82 mph sustained winds.

No damage/cleanup estimates were available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low dug into the western CONUS the 13th and moved inland over northern California and Nevada by the 17th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry thunderstorm developed across central Nevada and moved northward through the afternoon. The storm produced outflow winds over the Diamond Valley dry lake bed, which resulted in a dust storm. The dust storm moved into Elko and Spring Creek causing minor disruptions and impacts. The storm produced another dry microburst, resulting in a wind gust to 67 MPH in Elko and also produced minor damage throughout portions of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry microburst produced a wind gust to 67 MPH at the NWS Elko WFO. Other minor damage in town consisted of displaced shingles and a damaged fence. Blowing dust was also observed, but did not cause any significant impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds developed from a strong storm system moving through the Nevada during the days of April 15-16, 2018. Widespread strong winds were reported, but high winds were only recorded widespread across White Pine, southeastern Elko, and northeastern Nye Counties. Wind gusts in these areas were mostly in the 50-65 MPH range, with two locations seeing gusts to 70 MPH and 74 MPH. No reports of damage were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts occurred across White Pine County. Wind gusts ranged from 74 MPH at Mather RAWS in the Snake range to 58 MPH at the Ely Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and wet atmospheric river approached the west coast of the CONUS on the 5th, which brought gusty winds and significant rains to the Sierra and western Nevada the 6th through the 7th. In the foothills of western Nevada, rainfall amounts ranged from about a third of an inch to as much as three-quarters of an inch.  The valleys of the basin and range country in western Nevada saw generally less than half an inch, but the higher elevations received up to an inch or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained weather spotter DO-12 reported that approximately 4 inches of water was covering half of Nevada Highway 88 at Centerville Lane. This was very close to the location of Rocky Slough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low produced widespread showers and thunderstorms on May 15th, 2018. The showers and storms produced three separate funnel clouds across Lander and Humboldt Counties. The most notable funnel cloud occurred near Winnemucca, which garnered regional media attention as many people saw and took photos of the funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a cold core funnel cloud over the Jackson Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A well defined rope cold core funnel was reported by numerous spotters. The funnel cloud garnered regional media attention and was viewed by numerous people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low situated across northern Nevada produced isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms on May 11th, 2018. The showers and thunderstorms produced two separate tornadoes. The first tornado occurred south of Interstate 80 near Golconda in an open field. The second tornado touched down north of Golconda off U.S. Highway 95 and crossed a dirt road. No damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter saw the funnel cloud touchdown, picking up debris from an open field. The estimated width of the tornado was approximately 25 yards. This occurred in a rural location - no damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter saw a tornado briefly touchdown and move across a dirt road. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 16th and 17th, 2018 an upper level trough moved across northern Nevada, which produced showers and thunderstorms region wide. A few of the stronger storms became supercells over northeastern Nevada and produced hail, flash flooding, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel size hail. Photos showed significant hail accumulation impacting SR 225 north of Elko, Nevada.

The tornado was estimated at an elevation around 6500 feet to 7000 feet. Location was estimated based on radar. Due to the remote location of the tornado, no damage was observed or surveyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low slowly meandered across the Nevada from May 26th to the 28th, producing widespread showers and thunderstorms. The storms were slow movers, resulting in flash flooding across portions of the area. The storm system also spawned a powerful supercell, which moved from northeast to southwest north of Elko and Carlin. A funnel cloud and well developed wall cloud was observed. Due to the radar signature, significant hail, strong winds, and a tornado were likely, but mostly stayed away from populated areas and were not reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported via social media of water over Interstate 80 east of the Carlin Tunnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across Interstate 80 near Ryndon in Elko County on May 30th, 2018. The storms produced strong winds, with the highest wind gust at Coal Mine Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of upper level low pressure moved into the western part of the U.S. on the 16th and persisted through the 17th. This low brought greater forcing for showers and thunderstorms and produced a few strong storms across parts of Mineral County and the basin and range country on the 16th. The low also resulted in bands of moderate to heavy rain developing north of the main center of the low stretching across the northern Sierra and into far western Nevada on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP observer station CSVN2, Cold Springs Valley reported 1.16 inches of rainfall within a 24-hour period from 15 May 2300PST to 16 May 2300PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Perry Wildfire started on 27 July with the cause listed as unknown/under investigation. By 6 August, it had burned 51,400 acres and was fully contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed on the 4th due to a passing trough of low pressure. Gusts reached into the 35 to 45 mph range for many locations with mountaintop gusts in excess of 55 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of slow moving thunderstorms developed in central Nevada, causing localized heavy rain. The heaviest rainfall occurred in northern Nye County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over Duckwater, NV caused a debris flow over SR 379. The debris flow was estimated to be 5 to 7 feet wide and 2 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed over southern Washoe County during the early evening of the 19th producing brief heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet C3920, 2 miles north-northeast of Sparks (elevation 4596 feet) reported 0.27 inches of rainfall within 15 minutes from 19 May 1633PST to 1648PST and 0.55 inches of rainfall within an hour from 19 May 1618PST to 1718PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and somewhat moist winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of northern Elko county, as well as the mountain locations in the forecast area in central Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured fourteen inches at Vacarro Spring station, elevation 7836 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating and ample moisture along the periphery of a ridge caused scattered convection over western Nevada and the eastern Sierra July 19th through the 25th. Several storms produced heavy rainfall which led to flash flooding and debris flows. Hail was also reported with some storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station SCRN2, 5 miles northeast of Washoe City (elevation 4614 feet) reported 1.15 inches of rainfall over a 3 hour and 45 minute period from 21 July 1350PST to 1735PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed over southern Washoe County during the early evening of the 19th producing brief heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 0.50 inches of rainfall near the intersection of Prater and Sparks Blvd from 19 May 1605PST to 1705PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of upper level low pressure moved into the western part of the U.S. on the 16th and persisted through the 17th. This low brought greater forcing for showers and thunderstorms and produced a few strong storms across parts of Mineral County and the basin and range country on the 16th. The low also resulted in bands of moderate to heavy rain developing north of the main center of the low stretching across the northern Sierra and into far western Nevada on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station ALMN2, 5 miles southwest of Reno (elevation 6160 feet) reported 1.11 inches of rainfall in a 24-hour period from 16 May 0130PST to 17 May 0130PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak warm air advection created light snow across the Sierra and northeast California during the late night hours of the 3rd and early morning hours of the 4th. Additional light snow developed on the afternoon and evening of the 4th and extended into the morning of the 5th as a weak deformation axis developed over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP observer station VRCN2, Virginia City reported 8 inches of snow in a 24-hour period from 4 December 0800PST to 5 December 0800PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another low pressure system moved slowly from the central California coast to western Nevada on the 24th and 25th. This system produced two periods of showers and thunderstorms where rainfall totals were maximized across much of eastern California from Alpine County northward to Lassen County and western Nevada from Douglas County northward to the Oregon border. The first period occurred during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 24th, and the second occurred during the morning of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS station NV-WH-147, 6 miles south-southwest of Sparks reported 0.61 inches of rainfall in a 24-hour period from 24 May 0600PST to 25 May 0600PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nevada Department of Transportation reported a washout/water over the roadway at mile marker 55 on Nevada Highway 447 in Pershing County. Heavy rains from showers and thunderstorms likely came down an alluvial fan near either the Limbo or Tohakum 2 fire burn scar leading to the washout/water over the roadway. A damage estimate was not available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed over the forecast area with the strongest winds over northern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coal mine canyon (5550 feet).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four-hour snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches at lake level in the Tahoe Basin to as much as 6 inches at the higher elevations around Lake Tahoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 12, 2018, a strong push of monsoonal moisture moved into central Nevada. This led to thunderstorms, which produced flash flooding in northern Nye County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports via social media videos and pictures of street flooding in Tonopah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered over eastern Nevada brought bands of rain showers to western Nevada. Some of the stronger showers briefly kicked up northerly winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wave of rain showers passed through Pyramid Lake and brought northerly winds sustained to around 30 mph with gusts to 34 mph. A boater drowned at approximately 1030PST on 12 May when winds were determined to have peaked based on local observations near Sutcliffe along the shore of Pyramid Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low moved south over southern Nevada and persisted in that area through much of the 22nd, which caused showers and thunderstorms to become widespread across western Nevada and the eastern Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A collapsing core within a thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall over the Slinkard burn scar west of Topaz Lake, which caused two pronounced debris flows off the burn scar onto U.S. Highway 395 near the California/Nevada state line. The debris flows were up to 5 feet deep in a few places and closed the highway for over 18 hours while crews removed the flow. Nearly 200 people from the nearby Topaz Lodge were forced to the second floor as mud hit the exterior part of the first floor. No injuries were reported. No damage estimates were available from Topaz Lodge. The portable RAWS site above the Slinkard Fire burn scar (NCRC1; elevation 7451 feet) recorded 0.27 inches of rainfall in 15 minutes at the time two debris flows were reported (21 May 1727PST). A total of 0.90 inches of rainfall eventually fell on the burn scar in a two and a half hour time period from 21 May 1652PST to 1922PST. The cost to clear the debris flow for the Nevada Department of Transportation was a total of $51,476, which includes labor and equipment costs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over parts of the northern Lake Tahoe Basin, Verdi, and the Reno-Sparks Metropolitan area the 12th through the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station KNXN2, 1 mile north of Incline Village (elevation 7568 feet) reported 1.59 inches of rainfall during a 3-hour period from 12 July 1519PST to 1819PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak warm air advection created light snow across the Sierra and northeast California during the late night hours of the 3rd and early morning hours of the 4th. Additional light snow developed on the afternoon and evening of the 4th and extended into the morning of the 5th as a weak deformation axis developed over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four-hour snowfall amounts ranged from 1.4 inches at the Reno-Tahoe International Airport to 5 inches in northwest Reno and northern Reno. Spanish Springs and Sparks received between 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the 24 hour period. Significant numbers of traffic accidents occurred in the Reno-Sparks area when temperatures rapidly cooled below freezing during the evening rush hour of Dec 4th. Areas within Carson City received 3 to 3.5 inches of snowfall, while the southwest portion of the Carson Valley received anywhere from 3 to 5 inches of snowfall during the 24 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter 1 mile north-northwest of Incline Village reported 0.57 inches of rainfall in a 1-hour period from 12 July 1756PST to 1856PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of December started with a fast-moving upper level low and cold front sweeping east-southeast across north-central Nevada. These features brought snow to western Nevada and the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirty-six-hour snowfall amounts ranged from 2-4 inches at 6700 feet in Incline Village to as much as a 10 inches at higher elevations around Lake Tahoe (Mount Rose Ski Area).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Perry Wildfire started on the 27th with the cause listed as unknown/under investigation. By 5 August, it had burned 51,400 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Perry Fire southwest of Pyramid Lake burned 51,400 acres from 27 July 1622PST to 6 August 2100PST. Three residences and 15 other structures were damaged or destroyed. The fire cost at least $2.6 million dollars to fight. Microwave road was also closed during the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A number of destructive wildfires burned across central and northern Nevada in July, which had significant economic impacts. Some of the impacts included, closed roads and highways, infrastructure impacts, damaged outbuildings, impacts to grazing, watersheds, and evacuations. Some of the more dustructive fires were the Martin, Echo, Hogan, Goose Creek, Dixie, Stag, Silverstate, Boone Springs, HD, Gregg, and Antelope.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Martin Fire started in eastern Humboldt County and moved into western Elko County in northern Nevada. It became one of the largest fires in U.S. history with a final size at 435,569 acres. Impacts to grazing allotments, significant critical sage grouse habitat, and other unknown economic impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms ignited a large and destructive wildfire in northern Nevada. The fire was called the South Sugarloaf Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Sugarloaf Fire burned 233,462 acres and destroyed at least two campgrounds. Additional structures were also damaged or destroyed, but exact numbers are not known. In addition to the structures, critical sage-grouse habitat, grazing, livestock, and wildlife habitat was either damaged/destroyed or threatened. One firefighter also sustained injuries while battling the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and unstable airmass led to development of a damaging dust devil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong dust devil was observed picking up an anchored shed and tossing it over 50 yards. The shed was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced gusty outflow winds of unknown speed, which destroyed scaffolding in downtown Elko.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scaffolding collapsed from gusty outflow winds between 50 to 55 mph. No injuries, but the roadway was blocked. Elko Airport also gusted to 51 mph and NWS Elko gusted to 49 mph. Blowing dust was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain on December 6th followed by clearing skies and colder temperatures led to patchy dense fog around the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of spotters in and near Pahrump reported visibility of less than an eighth of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain on December 6th followed by clearing skies and colder temperatures led to patchy dense fog around the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reduced visibility to less than an eighth of a mile on I-15 between Jean and Sloan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds in the Las Vegas Valley led to an indirect fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A window washer fell to his death from the Trump Tower after wind gusts slammed him against the building. Some surface stations were reporting gusts of 20 to 30 mph at the time, but gusts on the side of the building could have been higher due to the elevation and funneling of the wind next to the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One man died in Las Vegas of cold related causes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system plus leftover moisture from the remnants of Hurricane Rosa produced thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Some storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding on Main Street in Pioche left water and debris in the street and water in several buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system plus leftover moisture from the remnants of Hurricane Rosa produced thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Some storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two sensors measured wind gusts to 60 and 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system pulled up a plume of subtropical moisture, triggering thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. One storm produced hail.
Gusty winds and dry conditions helped to fan a large and destructive fire in the Ruby Mountains. The fire was human caused and burned in the Humboldt Toiyabe National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large and destructive wildfire burned in the Humboldt Toiyabe National Forest located in the Ruby Mountains. The fire was fanned by gusty winds and dry conditions with a majority of the burned total acreage occurring September 30th, 2018. Several buildings were lost with most of one campground destroyed. Monetary losses are unknown at this time, however the cost of suppressing the fire was in the millions. The final acreage was 9,169.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough lifted across California and the Great Basin the 21st into the 23rd. This weather system brought high winds, rain, and snow to the Sierra Nevada and parts of western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet atop Peavine Peak recorded a wind gust of 82 mph at an elevation of 8260 feet MSL.
